---
title: "R for Medical Students"
subtitle: "Student National Medical Association | AMEC 2026"
host: "Student National Medical Association | AMEC 2026"
abstract: |
  If you've ever spent two hours cleaning a spreadsheet by hand, sent your PI a table you couldn't quite reproduce, or quietly Googled "how to do statistics in Excel" the night before a deadline, this talk is for you.
  
  At AMEC 2026, I led a 60-minute workshop where we wrote a clinical paper together, in real time, using R. Not a toy example. Actual manuscript outputs. We started with a de-identified RCT dataset, built a publication-ready Table 1 with automatic p-values, made two annotated results figures, and wrote the Results section text to go with them. Every attendee ran the code live in their browser. No installation, no setup, no prior R experience required.
  
  The goal wasn't to turn a room full of medical students into data scientists. It was to show that the jump from Excel to R is much shorter than it looks, and that reproducible, well-documented analysis isn't just a nice-to-have. It's becoming the expectation at submission.
  
  This workshop grew out of something I keep running into in my own research at the intersection of clinical data science and health equity: the tools that make research more rigorous are often framed as too technical for clinicians. I don't think that's true. I think most people just haven't seen what's possible in 60 minutes.

author: "Mirindi T. Kabangu"
date: "2026-04-01"
categories: [slides]
engine: knitr
---

Click [here](https://mkabangu.github.io/talks/2026-snma-coding-workshop/presentation.html) for a full screen presentation.

<iframe src="https://mkabangu.github.io/talks/2026-snma-coding-workshop/presentation.html#title-slide" width="100%" height="50%">

</iframe>
